one_line_takeaway: By moving redaction out of the answering LLM's prompt and into a separate, query-blind retrieval-time step, SD-RAG cuts privacy-score degradation under prompt injection roughly in half versus a monolithic-prompt RCQA baseline (extractive variant: 0.827->0.779 privacy score under attack vs. baseline's 0.502->0.199) at the cost of extra LLM calls and no protection against a poisoned/self-injecting corpus.
key_finding: Table I (privacy/completeness under prompt injection, averaged over strategy x injection presence): Baseline [11] privacy score 0.502 (no injection) drops to 0.199 (with injection); completeness 0.689 -> 0.583. SD-RAG 'Rephrase' (periphrastic): privacy 0.729 (no injection) -> 0.585 (injection); completeness 0.622 -> 0.584. SD-RAG 'Extract' (extractive): privacy 0.827 (no injection) -> 0.779 (injection); completeness 0.598 -> 0.582. Extractive redaction has both the highest privacy score and the smallest drop under attack; the monolithic baseline has a slightly higher completeness score in the benign case (up to ~10% higher) but a much larger privacy collapse under injection. The paper's headline number, 'up to a 58% improvement in privacy score,' is the comparison of SD-RAG's best configuration against the baseline under the optimal setting (per Conclusion). Re-ranking ablation: among five candidate-constraint re-ranking strategies (similarity-to-concatenated-chunks, similarity-to-query, max-similarity-to-chunk, average-similarity, and query-weighted average-similarity), the query-weighted average-similarity scheme performed best (Fig. 8), and plain averaging outperformed max-similarity because averaging dampens 'outlier constraints.' Hierarchical-summarization ablation (Table II, 3 settings over a 3-layer tree): leaves+all summaries [0,1,2] -> privacy 0.749/completeness 0.623; leaves only [0] -> privacy 0.739/completeness 0.637; summaries only [1,2] -> privacy 0.813/completeness 0.535 -- summarization gives only a modest privacy gain at a comparatively large completeness cost. Latency: extractive redaction was unexpectedly slower than periphrastic on both Qwen2.5-7B and Llama-3-8B (Fig. 9), attributed to the GBNF-grammar-constrained decoding used to force structured span-list output in the extractive redactor.
core_contribution: SD-RAG is a RAG architecture that moves privacy/selective-disclosure enforcement out of the generation prompt and into the retrieval pipeline, so that a prompt-injection attack against the final LLM call cannot bypass redaction. The system (1) lets a human data owner specify dynamic, natural-language privacy/security 'constraints'; (2) encodes the document corpus and these constraints as two node types (data nodes, constraint nodes) in a graph-based data model, linking constraints to chunks by semantic proximity ('constraint binding'); (3) at retrieval time, binds/re-ranks the top-K constraints relevant to the retrieved chunks and applies them via a dedicated redactor LLM call that never sees the user's (untrusted) query, using either 'extractive' redaction (detect-and-mask sensitive spans) or 'periphrastic' redaction (constraint-guided paraphrasing); (4) only then passes the sanitized context to the answering LLM. The paper also contributes a semi-automated synthetic dataset-construction pipeline for redaction-aware closed QA (RCQA) and two new evaluation metrics (Privacy Score, Completeness Score).
threat_model: Formalized in Section III-A. The user prompt p is untrusted and may contain adversarial prompt-injection instructions aiming to maximize leakage of the private context C supplied to the answering LLM (Eq. 1). The corpus/database D building context C is assumed trusted (small-organization setting with trusted write access). The redactor model LM_r is assumed not fine-tuned on sensitive data, so it cannot itself leak sensitive information (formalized via mutual information I(C;theta_LMr)=0, Eq. 3). Privacy constraints X are assumed mutually independent (no entailment enforcement across constraints, Eq. 4). Explicitly out of scope / stated limitations: a poisoned corpus (retrieved chunks themselves containing injected instructions) is not handled -- the paper assumes retrieved chunks are benign; multi-turn de-anonymization/inference attacks by an attacker with prior background knowledge of the corpus are not evaluated (illustrated with a 'John Doe' police-record example showing residual re-identification risk even when SD-RAG redacts correctly).
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors (Section V-A): (1) assumes the corpus is not poisoned -- a malicious retrieved chunk containing its own prompt injection could disable the pre-redaction step, and this scenario is not evaluated; (2) evaluation limited to small open-source quantized models (Qwen2.5 7B, Llama-3 8B, q4_k_m) due to hardware constraints; larger/closed-source models were not tested, though the authors argue benefits should generalize since the approach is retrieval/data-governance-layer and largely model-agnostic (this generalization claim is not empirically validated in the paper); (3) assumes the attacker has no prior background knowledge of the corpus -- multi-turn de-anonymization/inferential re-identification attacks are not studied, and the paper's own worked example (the 'John Doe' police-record case) shows SD-RAG's post-retrieval, per-answer redaction can still leak identity through correlation even when each individual constraint is technically satisfied; (4) redaction costs at least 2 LLM calls versus 1 for the monolithic baseline, adding latency (quantified in Fig. 9, though authors judge it 'not dramatic'); (5) constraints are assumed mutually independent with no mechanism to propagate logically entailed constraints (Eq. 4), a structural assumption not stress-tested with adversarial constraint sets.

datasets_used: Self-constructed synthetic dataset: 20 short synthetic articles across various sensitive-information topics/categories, each with annotated 'witness words' per constraint and golden Q&A pairs, built via a semi-automated 6-step pipeline using Gemini 2.5 Flash plus manual inspection for consistency
quantitative_results: Table I - Baseline [11]: privacy 0.502438 (no injection) -> 0.198961 (injection); completeness 0.689494 -> 0.583328
Table I - Rephrase (SD-RAG periphrastic): privacy 0.728657 -> 0.585128; completeness 0.621787 -> 0.583601
Table I - Extract (SD-RAG extractive): privacy 0.826739 -> 0.778937; completeness 0.597963 -> 0.582014
Headline claim: up to 58% improvement in privacy score over baseline under optimal configuration (per Abstract/Conclusion)

Table II - hierarchical summarization ablation: layers [0,1,2] privacy 0.748826 / completeness 0.623125; layers [0] privacy 0.738732 / completeness 0.637327; layers [1,2] privacy 0.813380 / completeness 0.534865

Re-ranking corpus stats: 20 articles, ~13,600 tokens, 639 chunks, 120 constraints, each constraint initially attached to top-50 similar chunks; evaluated via Recall@K and AP@K for K=1..9 across 5 re-ranking strategies (Figs. 4-8), best strategy = query-weighted average similarity
Latency (Fig. 9): extractive redaction slower than periphrastic redaction on both Qwen2.5-7B q4_k_m and Llama-3-8B q4_k_m on a Tesla T4 GPU (exact ms values only given graphically, not in text)
baselines_compared: Monolithic-prompt Redacted Contextual Question Answering (RCQA) baseline from Lichtefeld et al. 2024 (ref [11]), which embeds privacy constraints directly in the same prompt as the user's (potentially malicious) query
